name: Jarama Trails Loop
name_original: Presa de la Parra desde Pontón de la Oliva — circular por la Cañada de la Caleriza
description: Hike a moderate 10.1-mile (16.3 km) circular route from Pontón de la Oliva to Presa de la Parra, exploring river views and limestone cliffs.
region_intro: Hiking around Uceda offers diverse landscapes, from the panoramic Jarama Valley to dramatic limestone cliffs and unique geological formations. The region is characterized by its varied terrain, including river views and historical elements such as ancient walls and castle ruins. These features provide a compelling backdrop for exploration, with trails winding through both natural beauty and historical sites. The area presents a network of paths suitable for various fitness levels, from gentle walks to more challenging treks. Best hiking trails around Uceda The most popular hiking route is Cárcavas de Valdepeñas de la Sierra Loop, a 9.2 miles (14.8 km) trail that takes 4 hours 38 minutes to complete. This moderate route highlights distinctive erosive landscapes and unique geological formations. Another top favourite among local hikers is Pontón de la Oliva to La Parra Dam Circular Trail, a moderate 10.6 miles (17.0 km) path. This trail features stunning river views and dramatic limestone cliffs along the Jarama. Local hikers also love the Jarama Trails Loop, a 10.1 miles (16.3 km) trail leading through the expansive Jarama Valley, often completed in about 4 hours 40 minutes. Hiking around Uceda is defined by the panoramic Jarama Valley, impressive river features, and unique geological formations. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult trails, catering to different ability levels. The routes in Uceda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 300 reviews. More than 2000 hikers have used komoot to explore Uceda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Uceda?
A: Uceda offers a comprehensive network of over 100 hiking routes. These trails cater to various fitness levels, including easy, moderate, and difficult options, ensuring there's a suitable path for everyone.

Q: What types of terrain can I expect on hikes near Uceda?
A: Hikers in Uceda can expect diverse terrain, ranging from the expansive views of the Jarama Valley to dramatic limestone cliffs and unique geological formations like the Cárcavas. Many routes also feature river views and historical elements such as ancient walls and castle ruins.

Q: Are there hiking options for beginners or families in Uceda?
A: Yes, Uceda provides a good selection of easy trails, perfect for beginners or families. These routes typically involve less elevation gain and shorter distances, allowing for a relaxed exploration of the area's natural beauty.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Uceda?
A: The region's climate generally makes spring and autumn ideal for hiking in Uceda, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon hikes are recommended, while winters are typically mild but can be cooler.

Q: What natural features can I discover while hiking in Uceda?
A: Hiking around Uceda allows you to discover impressive natural features such as the panoramic Jarama Valley, stunning river views, and dramatic limestone cliffs. You can also explore unique geological formations like the distinctive erosive landscapes found on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022912375/carcavas-de-valdepenas-de-la-sierra-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cárcavas de Valdepeñas de la Sierra Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks to see on the trails around Uceda?
A: Yes, Uceda's historical context enriches the hiking experience. You can explore ancient walls and castle ruins within the town itself. Trails also lead to landmarks like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5442590" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pontón de la Oliva Dam and Cliffside Pathways&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4615324" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Town Gate of Talamanca de Jarama&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Uceda?
A: Absolutely. Many of Uceda's trails are circular, offering convenient starting and ending points. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e956893847/ponton-de-la-oliva-to-la-parra-dam-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pontón de la Oliva to La Parra Dam Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025831190/jarama-trails-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jarama Trails Loop&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to experience diverse scenery without retracing your steps.

Q: Are there any gorges or canyons to explore near Uceda?
A: Yes, the area around Uceda features interesting gorges and canyons. You can explore highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5440216" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañada de la Caleriza Trail&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4551346" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Cárcavas - Pontón de la Oliva&lt;/a&gt;, which offer unique geological formations and rugged beauty.

Q: Are the hiking trails around Uceda suitable for dogs?
A: Many trails in the Uceda region are suitable for dogs, offering open spaces and natural environments for your canine companions. However, it's always advisable to check specific trail regulations and ensure your dog is kept on a leash, especially in protected areas or near wildlife.

Q: Is there public transport available to reach hiking trailheads in Uceda?
A: While Uceda is accessible, public transport options directly to specific trailheads can be limited. It's often recommended to use a car for maximum flexibility to reach the diverse starting points of the hiking routes in the region.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Uceda?
A: The hiking trails in Uceda are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.7 stars from over 300 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the diverse landscapes, the well-maintained paths, and the stunning views of the Jarama Valley and its unique geological formations.

Q: Are there any summits or viewpoints with panoramic views in the Uceda area?
A: Yes, the Uceda region offers several points with excellent panoramic views. The town itself, particularly from higher elevations near the cemetery, provides stunning vistas of the Jarama Valley. For a more challenging ascent, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839855" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cancho de la Cabeza Summit&lt;/a&gt; offers expansive views of the surrounding landscape.
tips:
The Pontón de la Oliva dam, built during the reign of Isabel II, is the oldest dam in Madrid. It is built in limestone, the rock that we find in the surroundings.
Since the mid-eighteenth century there was already the idea of supplying water to the court from some of the mighty rivers of the Sierra de Madrid, such as the Lozoya. It is in the middle of the 19th century, when the olive pontoon was selected as the most suitable place to build the first dam in Madrid.

The Cañada de la Caleriza is a winding path that runs along the banks of the Lozoya River. This section coincides with the beginning of the GR 88, which runs through the provinces of Madrid, Segovia and Guadalajara. The Lozoya is a tributary of the Jarama along whose 91-kilometer route six reservoirs have been built.

The La Parra dam was built with the purpose of relieving the Navarejos dam, which is located downstream of the Lozoya River. Due to more recent works, today the dam is in disuse but forms part of the well-known La Parra Dam - Pontón de la Oliva route.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular hike?
faqs.answer: Parking is available at the starting point near the Pontón de la Oliva Dam. This area is easily accessible by car.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas where livestock might be grazing or to protect local wildlife. Please also be mindful of other hikers and pack out any waste.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers different charms throughout the year. Early spring brings vibrant green meadows, while late October is noted for the beautiful changing foliage of willows, ash trees, and poplars along the Lozoya River. Consider avoiding the hottest summer months due to exposure, and be prepared for varied conditions in winter.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit or to pay an entrance fee to hike this trail. It is located within the Parque Natural de la Sierra Norte de Guadalajara, which is freely accessible to visitors. However, always respect local regulations, especially regarding fire prevention and waste disposal.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied. You'll encounter approximately 12.8 kilometers of unpaved natural paths and dirt tracks, along with designated hiking paths, general paths, and shorter sections of paved road. Expect steep limestone walls, especially around the Lozoya river canyon, and sections that involve ascending and descending, such as crossing the Cabeza del Molino hill.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular hike?
faqs.answer: This circular route, which is approximately 16.3 km (10.1 miles) long, typically takes about 5-6 hours to complete. This estimate accounts for the moderate difficulty and elevation changes along the way.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or natural features will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several interesting features, including the historic Pontón de la Oliva Dam, the scenic Cañada de la Caleriza Trail, and the La Parra Weir. The route also follows the majestic Lozoya River, offering views of impressive limestone cliffs and a canyon, and passes through green meadows.

faqs.question: Is this a good trail for beginners?
faqs.answer: While generally rated as 'moderate' for hiking, requiring 'good fitness' and 'sure-footedness' for the full 16.3 km, it might be challenging for absolute beginners due to its length and some ascents/descents. Shorter, less strenuous sections exist along the Lozoya River if you're looking for an introductory experience with less elevation change.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other established hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with and includes sections of several established paths. Notably, you'll find parts of the GR-10 long-distance path, as well as sections of Senderos del Jarama, Senda 2: Remontando el Lozoya hasta la Presa de la Parra, and the Senda del Genaro.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's length and varied terrain, it's advisable to pack plenty of water, snacks, and wear sturdy hiking boots. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is recommended, especially on exposed sections. Depending on the season, layers of clothing are useful, and a map or GPS device is always a good idea for navigation.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: The full 16.3 km circular route might be too long and challenging for younger children or families not accustomed to longer hikes. However, shorter, linear sections along the Lozoya River with minimal elevation change could be suitable for families looking for a more relaxed outing.

waypoints.name: Pontón de la Oliva Dam and Cliffside Pathways
